summary: I was born in Buhl, Idaho, where my parents had moved from Tennessee shortly after World War I. They were among many who relocated there because of new reclamation projects and water-irrigated land. My father initially worked for a farmer before joining the Home Lumber and Coal Company in Buhl. My siblings and I were all born in Buhl before we moved to Boise when I was three.

My parents originally came from Tennessee before moving to Buhl, Idaho, soon after World War I. They were the first of several family members to make that move, seeking new opportunities with the irrigation projects in Idaho. Though I was born in Idaho, my family's roots trace back to Tennessee, which shaped part of my early family history.

World War I indirectly influenced my family's move from Tennessee to Idaho. After the war, many people, including my parents, relocated to places like Buhl because of new agricultural opportunities created by reclamation and irrigation projects. This post-war period shaped the environment I was born into.

My father worked for the Home Lumber and Coal Company first in Buhl and then became the manager of their Boise branch after we moved there in 1924. This company was central to my family's livelihood during my early years. It was through his work there that we relocated to Boise, where I grew up and attended school.

We moved to Boise, Idaho, when I was about three years old, around 1924, after my father took a managerial position at the Home Lumber and Coal Company there. I grew up in Boise and attended local schools, graduating from Boise High School in 1939. Later, I attended Boise Junior College, where I also participated in the Civilian Pilot Training program.

I graduated from Boise High School in 1939 after growing up in Boise. It was the foundation of my education before I moved on to Boise Junior College. My time there was important as it led to my involvement in aviation training programs.

I started at Boise Junior College in the fall after high school, attending the old building near St. Luke's Hospital for my first year. The next year, I was there when they opened the new campus at the old Boise airport. It was during my sophomore year at Boise Junior College that the war broke out, and I was also training in the Civilian Pilot Training program on campus.
cues: old building near St. Luke's Hospital

summary: The old building of Boise Junior College where I took my first year was located near St. Luke's Hospital in Boise. This proximity was part of my early college experience before the school moved to the airport campus. St. Luke's was a landmark near my initial college days.

I vividly remember the day Pearl Harbor was attacked. It was a Sunday when my two best friends and I went to the Pinney Theatre, and the box office girl told us the news had just come over the air. That moment made it clear that the war would deeply affect our lives, and it was shortly after that I joined the Civilian Pilot Training program.

I was part of the Civilian Pilot Training program while at Boise Junior College, flying off the campus. We would taxi down near Broadway and take off right over the Student Union Building. This program was my first real flight training, and it prepared me for entering the Navy's aviation program in 1942.

The Pinney Theatre in Boise is where I was with my two best buddies when we heard the news of the Pearl Harbor attack. The girl at the box office, whom we knew, told us the news had just come over the air. That moment at the Pinney Theatre is etched in my memory as the start of a new chapter in my life.

My initial flight training was in Piper Cubs during the Civilian Pilot Training program at Boise Junior College. My instructor was Val Berrichola from Napa, and this training took place in the spring of 1942. Flying the Piper Cub was my first real experience in the air and set the stage for my Navy aviation career.

I was sworn into the Navy in Seattle in the summer of 1942 after completing my primary flight training. Seattle was the place where my official Navy aviation career began, marking a transition from civilian training to military service.

After joining the Navy, my first training was at St. Mary's College of California, where we underwent a rigorous three-month pre-flight physical conditioning program. The training included football, basketball, swimming, and other sports to prepare us physically before we ever flew an airplane. This was in 1942 and was a tough but essential part of becoming a Naval aviator.

Following pre-flight training, I went to Los Alamitos near Long Beach, California, for flight training in the Yellow Perils and Stearman open-cockpit trainers. I was there for about three months in 1942, and one of the highlights was escorting Bob Hope around the base. Los Alamitos was where I learned to fly in earnest and experienced the intense pace of military flight training.

While training at Los Alamitos, I had the unique experience of escorting Bob Hope around the base before one of his shows. He was incredibly clean-living, which surprised me given his comedic reputation. That hour with him was a real highlight during my flight training in 1942.

Kay Kyser was another entertainer who visited during my training, and he was known for being quite foul-mouthed, which contrasted with Bob Hope's clean comedy. I remember hearing about him during my time at Los Alamitos, though I didn't have a personal encounter like with Bob Hope.

During my boxing days at Boise Junior College, I sparred with tough guys from places like Mountain Home Air Force Base. They were some of the toughest opponents I faced, and it showed me the level of physical conditioning expected of military trainees in the area. Mountain Home was part of the local military presence that influenced my training environment.
cues: Mountain Home Air Force Base opponents

summary: Gowen Field was another nearby military base whose trainees I encountered during my boxing matches at Boise Junior College. The competition was fierce, and it was clear that the military training at Gowen Field prepared men well physically. This base was part of the military community surrounding Boise during my early training years.

After completing flight training in California, I went to Beeville, Texas, for further training in the Vultee Vibrator aircraft. Beeville was close to Kingsville and Corpus Christi, and it was where I transitioned from primary trainers to more advanced aircraft. This was part of my progression toward becoming a Naval aviator in 1943.
cues: Beeville near Kingsville and Corpus Christi

summary: Kingsville, Texas, was near Beeville, where I trained on the Vultee Vibrator. It was part of the cluster of training bases including Corpus Christi, where I completed my advanced flight training. Kingsville was a key location in my path to earning my wings.

I graduated and earned my wings at Corpus Christi, Texas, after training on the AT-6 Texan. Corpus Christi was where I completed my advanced flight training and made the decision to become a fighter pilot rather than a bomber or torpedo pilot. It was a pivotal point in my military aviation career in 1943.

The Vultee Vibrator was the first aircraft I trained on after leaving California, at Beeville, Texas. It was an important step before moving on to the AT-6 Texan. Though it was a basic trainer, it helped prepare me for more advanced flying challenges.

The AT-6 Texan was the aircraft I trained on at Corpus Christi, Texas, and I considered it the best trainer I flew. It was a step up from the Vultee Vibrator and the Stearman, and it prepared me well for fighter operations. I earned my wings flying the AT-6 in 1943.

At Boise Junior College in the fall of 1942, we flew the Ryan ST, an open-cockpit airplane that was excellent for acrobatics. It was part of the Civilian Pilot Training program, and although it sometimes cut out when inverted, it was one of my favorite planes to fly before joining the Navy.

For carrier landing training, I flew the F4F Wildcat on Lake Michigan in winter gear during late 1943. The conditions were harsh and cold, and the cockpit was open, which made training challenging. This was my introduction to carrier operations before deployment.

We conducted carrier landing training on Lake Michigan in the winter of 1943. The cold weather made it difficult, especially since we flew open-cockpit F4F Wildcats and couldn't close the cockpit in freezing temperatures. This training was crucial for preparing us for carrier operations during the war.

entity: German submarine campaign of World War II
summary: The German submarine campaign off the coast of Florida caused the Navy to move carrier landing training to Lake Michigan for safety. This campaign influenced where and how we trained in 1943, as the threat of subs made coastal training risky. It was part of the broader challenges faced during World War II.
